skill.name: codebase-explorer
skill.description: Explore the Empathy Ledger codebase by mapping architecture, data flows, schema files, services, API routes, and component relationships.
skill.summary: Find where Empathy Ledger code lives and how its database, services, APIs, and components connect.
skill.author: Acurioustractor

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found shell, network, filesystem, and weak cryptography indicators, but review shows they are documentation examples and markdown references. No prompt injection, malware behavior, credential access, or data exfiltration intent was found in SKILL.md. The remaining risk is low because the skill suggests local code exploration commands and references project files.

low_findings.title: Markdown Paths Misidentified As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged inline backticks in tables as Ruby or shell execution. These lines list project file paths for database types and Supabase clients, and they are not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is inside markdown table cells and uses backticks only for inline code formatting. There is no interpreter invocation or user-controlled execution path.

low_findings.title: Code Examples Misidentified As Active Commands
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged fenced data-flow and TypeScript examples as command execution and network behavior. The fetch call is a conceptual example of an application API route, not a live request made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content appears inside documentation code fences that illustrate architecture. No executable script, endpoint, credential, or external network destination is present.

low_findings.title: Local Exploration Commands Require User Context
low_findings.description: The skill documents local commands such as ls, cat, grep, and tail for codebase exploration. These are legitimate for a code navigation skill, but users should expect the assistant may inspect project files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are simple read-only exploration commands and contain no destructive flags or remote transfer. The low residual risk comes from intentional local filesystem inspection.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Link Misidentified As Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The relative path to ARCHITECTURE_REFERENCE.md was flagged as path traversal. It is a markdown link to project documentation and is not used in file access code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The traversal sequence appears only in a markdown hyperlink. No code consumes the path or combines it with user input.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Has No Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported a weak cryptographic algorithm at the description line. Review found no cryptographic algorithm, hashing call, encryption code, or related instruction on that line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is frontmatter describing the skill purpose. It contains no MD5, SHA1, cipher selection, or cryptographic operation.

content.value_statement: Large codebases make it hard to find the right files and understand data flow. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Empathy Ledger architecture, schema, services, APIs, and components.
actual_capabilities: Maps database domains to their TypeScript type files and key tables.
Explains the standard flow from React components through API routes, services, Supabase, and PostgreSQL.
Identifies core service files and their business purpose.
Lists important API route families for stories, projects, transcripts, embeds, and administration.
Guides local searches for services, routes, migrations, table usage, and feature components.
Summarizes multi-tenant isolation and role hierarchy concepts documented in the skill.
limitations: It is specific to the Empathy Ledger repository layout described in SKILL.md.
It does not execute migrations, modify source files, or verify runtime behavior by itself.
It depends on the current repository matching the documented paths.
It provides navigation guidance, not a full security or performance audit.

use_cases.title: Onboard A New Developer
use_cases.description: Help a developer understand where schema, services, API routes, and components are located before making changes.

use_cases.title: Trace A Feature Flow
use_cases.description: Follow a feature from a user action through API routes, service logic, tenant checks, and database tables.

use_cases.title: Plan A Refactor
use_cases.description: Identify related files and system boundaries before changing services, database types, or route behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Find A Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Where is the story distribution feature implemented in this codebase? Show the main files and explain what each one does.

prompt_templates.title: Trace Data Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Trace how a story consent update moves from the UI to the database. Include routes, services, types, and tenant checks.

prompt_templates.title: Map Service Boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Map the service layer for story lifecycle management. Identify responsibilities, related tables, and likely integration points.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare A Change Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to add a new transcript review workflow. Find the relevant files, explain the existing patterns, and propose an implementation plan.

output_examples.input: Where do project transcripts connect to API routes and types?
output_examples.output: The response identifies the project transcript route family, related database type files, likely service boundaries, and table relationships. It also explains what to inspect next.

output_examples.input: How does tenant isolation work in this codebase?
output_examples.output: The response explains the tenant lookup pattern, where filters are expected, and why service-role access needs extra care.
The response summarizes relevant services, API routes, consent proof tables, revocation behavior, and component search locations.

best_practices: Ask for one feature or domain at a time so the file map stays focused.
Request file locations, relationships, and next inspection steps in the same prompt.
Verify documented paths against the current repository before editing code.
anti_patterns: Do not use it as proof that tenant isolation is correctly implemented.
Do not assume documented paths are complete after major repository changes.
Do not ask it to change code before first mapping the affected services and routes.

faq.question: What repository is this skill designed for?
faq.answer: It is designed for the Empathy Ledger codebase and its documented source layout.

faq.question: Can it modify source code?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides exploration guidance. Any edits must be requested separately.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No. The fetch text in SKILL.md is an architecture example, not a live network call.

faq.question: Why does it mention shell commands?
faq.answer: It lists read-only local commands that help locate services, API routes, migrations, and table usage.

faq.question: Can it help with Supabase architecture?
faq.answer: Yes. It points to Supabase clients, database type files, migrations, tenant filters, and related service patterns.

faq.question: Is this a general code search skill?
faq.answer: No. It is tailored to Empathy Ledger and may be inaccurate for unrelated repositories.
